name: Mutton Curry
tagline: Slow-cooked goat curry — heme iron, B12, and zinc in restaurant-style gravy
highlight: Bhuna technique — slow-fry the masala till oil separates twice

hero.alt: Dark-red mutton curry with bone-in goat pieces, oil pooling on top
hero.caption: mutton curry — slow-bhuna, deep-spiced

healthFacts: Goat meat: 3.7mg heme iron per 100g — absorbed 3× better than plant iron.
Single serving covers 100% of B12 and zinc daily targets.
Lower fat than lamb or mutton (sheep) — leaner red meat option.
Bone-in cuts release collagen and trace minerals into the gravy.

steps.title: Marinate the meat
steps.detail: Mix yogurt with half the ginger-garlic paste, ½ tsp salt, and a pinch of turmeric. Coat the goat pieces and rest 30 minutes (overnight in fridge for best result).
steps.tip: Yogurt's lactic acid pre-tenderises the meat — non-negotiable for goat.

steps.title: Brown the masala
steps.detail: Heat mustard oil till it just smokes. Add bay leaf and cinnamon. Brown sliced onions deeply (12 min). Add remaining ginger-garlic, then tomato purée. Cook till oil separates. Add chili, coriander, garam masala. Cook 2 more minutes.
steps.tip: Deeply browned onions are the soul of mutton curry — don't rush this.

steps.title: Bhuna and pressure-cook
steps.detail: Add the marinated goat and cook on medium-high for 10 minutes, stirring constantly — this is the bhuna. Add 3 cups water, transfer to a pressure cooker, cook 25 minutes (8–10 whistles). Open, simmer to thicken if needed. Finish with fresh garam masala.
steps.tip: Pressure cooker is the only practical way for goat — slow simmer takes 2.5 hours.
